methodology: Articles sourced from Virginia-specific RSS feeds and Google News RSS, filtered for AI/technology/workforce relevance, then assessed by Claude against the scenario framework. Each article is tagged with the region it most affects and the scenario it signals toward.

signals.title: New partnership between VCU and AI Ready RVA aims to boost AI education, innovation and community impact - VCU News
signals.signal_reading: VCU's formalized partnership with AI Ready RVA signals institutional concentration of AI education capacity in the Richmond metro hub, creating a regional center of excellence that could either become a distribution node or reinforce metro-concentrated capability.

signals.title: EPA grant backs community-driven workforce training in Southwest Virginia - Virginia Tech News
signals.signal_reading: EPA-funded community-driven workforce training in Southwest Virginia represents grassroots capacity-building in a historically underserved region, signaling potential for distributed deep learning if training focuses on technical skills rather than platform adoption.

signals.title: NoVA School Systems Grapple with AI’s Role in the Classroom - Northern Virginia Magazine
signals.signal_reading: NoVA schools grappling with AI in classrooms indicates early-stage platform adoption where students interact with AI tools rather than building them, suggesting shallow but broad distribution within the state's wealthiest region that could set statewide patterns.

signals.title: Norfolk Southern deploys AI system to catch wheel defects before they break - The Business Journals
signals.signal_reading: Norfolk Southern's deployment of specialized AI defect detection systems demonstrates deep technical capability in Hampton Roads' industrial sector, but as enterprise-level implementation rather than distributed workforce capacity, it reinforces concentrated expertise.

signals.title: AI is shaping health care. Maryland and Virginia are regulating it very differently - WAMU
signals.signal_reading: Virginia's hands-off regulatory approach to healthcare AI, contrasted with Maryland's active governance, signals policy drift that could allow capability concentration without mechanisms to ensure either depth or distribution across regions.
